issue.summary: The `snippet` field is mis-captured — every fixture finding reported snippet "requires login" instead of the actual sink line.
issue.description: On arms A and B the recon agent received findings whose `snippet` was the string "requires login" rather than the cited code (e.g. server.ts:25 `fs.writeFileSync(dest, content);`). The agent had to open the file to recover the real line, defeating the purpose of the snippet. semgrep's `extra.lines` is not being extracted/aligned to the matched line.
issue.reproduction: python tools/semgrep_run.py /tmp/cf-fixtures/armA-mcp → finding rule_id=chain-arbitrary-write-ts file=server.ts line=25 snippet="requires login" (wrong; real line is fs.writeFileSync(...))
issue.resolution: Prefer the matched line range (start.line..end.line) and, if `extra.lines` is empty/mismatched, fall back to reading the file at the cited line.
issue.update_note: Fix snippet extraction; this is a code fix, not a skill change.

issue.summary: Returns empty (0 nodes/edges) on MCP targets — its promotion-edge model misses pure tool-arg → filesystem-write primitives.
issue.description: On arm A (MCP server) and on src/gateway, agent_trust_graph emitted nodes=[]/edges=[]/promotions=0. The strongest finding (CHAIN-001, an unauthenticated tool-arg → arbitrary-write → RCE) had ZERO trust-graph support. The graph models trust-LAYER promotions (system/user/tool) but not the "untrusted MCP tool argument reaches a dangerous sink" boundary, which is the most common agent RCE entry.
issue.reproduction: python tools/agent_trust_graph.py /tmp/cf-fixtures/armA-mcp → {"nodes":[],"edges":[]} (no MCP tool-arg → sink edge)
issue.root_cause: Promotion-edge-centric design: it looks for low-trust content rising into a higher-trust message layer, not for tool-arg → exec/write/path sinks.
issue.resolution: Add tool-handler-arg nodes (USER/TOOL tier) and edges from them to file/exec/path sinks, so chaining (Phase 3-C) has graph support for tool-arg-to-sink primitives.
issue.update_note: Model MCP/tool-arg → sink edges, not only trust-layer promotions.

issue.summary: chain-write-to-exec-location over-fires on comments/object-literals; chain-arbitrary-write-ts tags bounded-write and test-helper hardcoded writes.
issue.description: In src/gateway, chain-write-to-exec-location (generic regex) matched a JSDoc comment (config-reload.ts:39) and an object-literal field (auth.ts:251); chain-arbitrary-write-ts matched hardcoded-content writes in a live-test helper. On arm B it tagged server.ts:27 as arbitrary-write though path.basename() (server.ts:25) makes it bounded-write. These are acceptable as low-confidence "primitive hints" (recon must read to confirm), but the noise is high on a large real tree.
issue.root_cause: Pure pattern-regex with no comment/context guard; arbitrary-write rule cannot see a downstream basename() sanitizer.
issue.resolution: Keep confidence LOW (already), but add comment-exclusion to the exec-location regex; document in semgrep-interpret.md that chain_primitive tags are hints requiring a manual read (already the design, restate).
issue.update_note: State explicitly that chain_primitive tags are low-confidence hints; recon MUST read the sink and check for downstream sanitizers before drawing an edge.

issue.summary: Empty approval_gates/sandbox_sites give no "safe-vs-absent" signal; mislabels generic registry.invoke()/WS test helpers as langchain LLM calls.
issue.description: On src/gateway, architecture_map returned tool_registry but empty approval_gates/sandbox_sites/memory_stores/sub_agent_spawners, and tagged generic registry.invoke(...) and WS test helpers (e.g. tools-invoke-http.cron-regression.test.ts:123) as llm_call_sites=langchain. An empty approval_gates list cannot distinguish "no gate exists" (a vuln) from "tool not scanned", weakening criterion ②.
issue.reproduction: python tools/architecture_map.py /tmp/crayfisher-.../src/gateway → approval_gates:[] llm_call_sites:[... mislabeled langchain ...]
issue.root_cause: Heuristic substring matching for LLM calls; no MCP/gateway approval-gate patterns; absence encoded the same as "not present".
issue.resolution: Distinguish "scanned, none found" from "not scanned"; add MCP/gateway approval-gate signatures; tighten llm_call_site heuristics to avoid test helpers.

issue.summary: Returns 0 entries on MCP server.tool handlers and the gateway WS/HTTP control plane.
issue.description: find_entries returned {"count":0,"entries":[]} for both the MCP fixtures and src/gateway. The attacker entry surface (MCP `server.tool` handlers; the WS connect handshake in server/ws-connection/message-handler.ts) was located manually via grep. Missing entry enumeration weakens criterion ①.
issue.reproduction: python tools/find_entries.py /tmp/cf-fixtures/armA-mcp → count:0 python tools/find_entries.py /tmp/crayfisher-.../src/gateway → count:0
issue.root_cause: Entry patterns target HTTP route decorators / CLI argparse, not MCP `server.tool(...)` registrations or gateway RPC/WS handlers.
issue.resolution: Add MCP tool-registration and WS/RPC handler patterns to find_entries.

issue.summary: `ulimit -v 8GiB` killed semgrep on WSL2 (large virtual-address mapping) → false INCONCLUSIVE; fixed by using semgrep --max-memory instead.
issue.description: The G-TOOLHEALTH probe's `ulimit -v 8388608` aborted semgrep even though semgrep_run.py succeeded, producing a false INCONCLUSIVE on arms A/B. ulimit -v bounds virtual address space, which semgrep maps large; the correct memory bound is semgrep's own --max-memory (MB).
issue.reproduction: (old) ulimit -v 8388608; semgrep scan ... → rc!=0 → INCONCLUSIVE (new) semgrep scan --max-memory 4000 --quiet ... → FINDINGS, stderr empty
issue.root_cause: Wrong memory knob for semgrep on this platform.
issue.resolution: Replaced ulimit -v with `--max-memory 4000 --quiet`; rely on `timeout` for wall-clock. Already applied.
